issues.title: Client certificates
issues.author: yaronf
issues.body:
From EKR:

In S 3.1 you say that this can be used for both server authentication
and client authentication, but the setting isn't really symmetrical.
S 3.2 says:

   *  The peer SHOULD include the cached algorithm in the
      signature_algorithms extension of its ClientHello (or
      CertificateRequest for servers), and MUST NOT include legacy (non-
      PQC) algorithms.

I don't see how this works: the client (usually) knows the server's
identity in advance but the server usually does not know the client's
in advance, so how does it know whether it has cached information or
not prior to receiving the client's certificate. One might attempt to
resolve this by just saying that the server should send its ordinary
signature_algorithms and then reject any non-PQ algorithms from the
client, but I don't think that quite works as-is because the client
might initially prefer PQ and then later not prefer PQ leading to a
failure. At least, I think you would need a warning that you shouldn't
advertise this extension as a client unless you are committing to
always sending a PQ cert if possible.

comments.author: yaronf

issues.title: Certificate chains
issues.author: yaronf
issues.body: Mention that the entire chain must be PQ-signed for PQ resistance, even if this draft cannot mandate it.

issues.title: Add a section about CDNs and middleboxes
issues.author: yaronf
issues.body:
CDNs: they are provisioned by the origin server, and it's possible that different CDNs present different chains for the same server. We could say that a server MUST NOT commit to PQC unless all legacy certs are expired/revoked.

Middleboxes: assuming middleboxes are incentivized NOT to break traffic, we could say that middleboxes MUST NOT commit to anything. A problem remains if a MB is a PQC client but not PQC server and the origin server already committed. (This breakage would apply to an HSTS solution, too).

In any case, commitments are cached per origin server  (DNS name) and not the whole chain, because an attacker replaces the whole chain.

comments.author: yaronf
comments.body: The most important point is that recovery is super-easy - just get a PQC cert for the server and install it. This would recover everybody other than clients that are behind a misbehaving middlebox.

issues.title: Design alternatives - add a section/appendix?
issues.author: yaronf
issues.body:
## Trust Anchors

* In large orgs, DNS is maintained by a different group, so not very convenient to server operators.
* The DNS record only includes trust anchors. There is nothing to say that trust anchors must be associated with a single algorithm, or even a family of algorithms that's either PQC or non-PQC but not both.
* With Trust Anchors, a client can still accept a certificate associated with a local anchor, even if that anchor was not published by the server. So rollback is still possible.

## HSTS

* Obviously this is only good for HTTP, not other TLS-based protocols.
* The main issue with pinning (middleboxes can cause breakage) is still there. This can be a malicious or a breached middlebox.
* Mixing layers, e.g. the app layer must be aware of the exact list of PQ algorithms.
* An attacker who has taken over the origin server can brick it with HSTS, just like with PQC-Continuity.
* Unlike PQC-C, HSTS is potentially vulnerable to application-level attacks (e.g. attacks initiated from the client side with XSS), where response splitting is used to cause [poisoning](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Cache_Poisoning?utm_source=chatgpt.com) of the client-side cache.

issues.title: Remove Signature Algorithm field from the extension
issues.author: yaronf
issues.body: Since the draft allows the server to move between different PQC algorithms, this field adds parsing complexity and provides little or no benefit. Drop.

issues.title: Implementation Status section
issues.author: yaronf
issues.body: Add an "Implementation Status" section to the draft per RFC 7942 (BCP 205).

issues.title: Malformed extension data
issues.author: yaronf
issues.body: When the CT ext is malformed, client must abort the handshake with `decode_error`.

reviews.author: yaronf
reviews.body: Looks good, other than one comment.

comments.body: I prefer the original text here, because a client may connect through a MITM for a few times, but once it connects directly even once, it obtains correct information.

pulls.title: Language consistency improvements
pulls.author: yaronf
pulls.body:
- Standardize terminology: use "PQC" consistently instead of "PQ"
- Standardize MitM capitalization (was "MiTM")
- Fix field name references: use `algorithm_validity_period` consistently
- Add code formatting to `pq_cert_available` identifier
- Fix RFC 2119 keyword: change "should" to "SHOULD"
- Add missing comma in abstract after "TLS layer"
- Improve clarity: "this extension" instead of "the extension"
- Complete IANA section: add "registry." to request sentence
- Fix IANA table spacing: "CH, CR, CT" (was "CH, CR,CT")

pulls.title: Technical consistency improvements
pulls.author: yaronf
pulls.body:
## Summary

This PR addresses technical consistency issues identified in the draft:

- **Bidirectional scope clarification**: Abstract and introduction now explicitly state the mechanism works peer-to-peer, with typical use being client-caching-server but also supporting server-caching-client
- **Cache update logic**: Clarified that when switching algorithms, use the new validity period from the extension
- **Zero value semantics**: Defined that `algorithm_validity_period` of zero means don't cache (if no cache exists) or clear cache (if exists)
- **Validation requirements**: Specified that `signature_algorithm` must match certificate and must be a PQC algorithm
- **Extension presence logic**: Clarified that when using traditional (non-PQC) certificate, sender should send extension with no data to indicate support
- **Commitment timing**: Clarified that duration is measured from the current TLS handshake

## Changes

All changes improve clarity and consistency without changing the fundamental protocol design.
